questionText: In how many years will $\text{Rs.}~150$ produce the same interest @ $8\%$ as $\text{Rs.}~800$ produce in 3 years @ $4\dfrac{1}{2}\%$?

steps.explanation: Find the SI produced by Rs. 800 at 4.5% for 3 years.
Now find T such that Rs. 150 at 8% produces SI = Rs. 108.

solution.shortcut: Equate SIs: $150 \times 8 \times T = 800 \times 4.5 \times 3 \Rightarrow T = 9$
solution.commonMistake: Using the wrong rate (e.g., 4% instead of 4.5%) for the first computation.

modelName: Simple Interest — Equal SI comparison

questionText: The simple interest accrued on a certain principal in 5 years at the rate of $12\%$ p.c.p.a. is $\text{Rs.}~1536$. What amount of simple interest would one get if one invests $\text{Rs.}~1000$ more than the previous principal for 2 years and at the same rate p.c.p.a.?

steps.explanation: Find the original principal from SI = Rs. 1536, R = 12%, T = 5 years.
New principal = 2560 + 1000 = Rs. 3560. Find SI at 12% for 2 years.
Rs. 854.40 does not match any of options A–D, so the answer is E) None of these.

solution.shortcut: First recover P from given SI, then compute new SI directly.
solution.commonMistake: Adding Rs. 1000 to SI instead of principal.

modelName: Simple Interest — Find principal then new SI

questionText: If $\text{Rs.}~64$ amounts to $\text{Rs.}~83.20$ in 2 years, what will $\text{Rs.}~86$ amount to in 4 years at the same rate percent per annum?

steps.explanation: Find rate: P = 64, SI = 83.20 − 64 = 19.20, T = 2 years.
Find SI on Rs. 86 at 15% for 4 years.
Amount = P + SI = 86 + 51.60.

solution.shortcut: Find rate from first case, apply to second case.
solution.commonMistake: Using T = 2 for the second case instead of T = 4.

modelName: Simple Interest — Rate transfer between cases

questionText: The simple interest on a certain sum of money at the rate of $5\%$ p.a. for 8 years is $\text{Rs.}~840$. At what rate of interest can the same amount of interest be received on the same sum after 5 years?

steps.explanation: Find the principal: SI = 840, R = 5%, T = 8 years.
Find rate so that P = 2100 gives SI = 840 in 5 years.

solution.shortcut: Since same SI needed in fewer years, the rate must increase proportionally.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing SI with amount when finding the principal.

modelName: Simple Interest — Find rate for same SI in different time

questionText: The interest on a certain deposit at $4.5\%$ p.a. is $\text{Rs.}~202.50$ in one year. How much will the additional interest in one year be on the same deposit at $5\%$ p.a.?

steps.explanation: Find principal: SI = 202.50, R = 4.5%, T = 1 year.
New SI at 5% for 1 year on Rs. 4500.
Additional interest = 225 − 202.50.

solution.shortcut: Additional interest = P × (5% − 4.5%) × 1 = 4500 × 0.5% = Rs. 22.50
solution.commonMistake: Selecting Rs. 225 (new SI) instead of the additional interest.

modelName: Simple Interest — Additional interest with new rate

questionText: A sum invested at $5\%$ simple interest per annum grows to $\text{Rs.}~504$ in 4 years. The same amount at $10\%$ simple interest per annum in $2\dfrac{1}{2}$ years will grow to:

steps.explanation: Let principal = x. Amount = 504, so SI = 504 − x. At 5% for 4 years: (x × 5 × 4)/100 = 504 − x.
Find SI on Rs. 420 at 10% for 2.5 years.
Amount = 420 + 105.

solution.shortcut: Recover principal first, then apply to new scenario.
solution.commonMistake: Treating the amount Rs. 504 as the principal.

modelName: Simple Interest — Recover principal then new amount

questionText: If $x$, $y$, $z$ are three sums of money such that $y$ is the simple interest on $x$, and $z$ is the simple interest on $y$ for the same time and at the same rate of interest, then:

steps.explanation: Let rate = R% and time = T years. Then y = xRT/100 ... (i)
Similarly z = yRT/100, so RT/100 = z/y ... (ii)
From the two equal ratios: y/x = z/y ⟹ y² = xz.

solution.shortcut: Both ratios of consecutive terms equal RT/100, so the sequence is a GP: y² = xz.
solution.commonMistake: Choosing x² = yz by reversing the ratio.

modelName: Simple Interest — Algebraic relationship between three sums

questionText: What will be the ratio of simple interest earned by a certain amount at the same rate of interest for 6 years and that for 9 years?

steps.explanation: SI is proportional to time when P and R are the same.

solution.shortcut: Ratio of SIs = ratio of times = 6 : 9 = 2 : 3.
solution.commonMistake: Thinking principal or rate is needed — they cancel in the ratio.

modelName: Simple Interest — Ratio of SIs for different times

questionText: Arun borrowed a sum of money from Jayant at the rate of $8\%$ per annum simple interest for the first four years, $10\%$ per annum for the next 6 years and $12\%$ per annum for the period beyond 10 years. If he pays a total of $\text{Rs.}~12160$ as interest only at the end of 15 years, how much money did he borrow?

steps.explanation: Let sum = Rs. x. Total SI = SI for 4 yrs at 8% + SI for 6 yrs at 10% + SI for 5 yrs at 12%.

solution.commonMistake: Using 15 years for the last period instead of 5 years (beyond 10 years up to 15).

modelName: Simple Interest — Multiple rates over different periods

questionText: Kruti took a loan at simple interest rate of $6\%$ p.c.p.a. in the first year and it increased by $1.5\%$ p.c.p.a. every year. If she pays $\text{Rs.}~8190$ as interest at the end of 3 years, what was her loan amount?

steps.explanation: Rates: Year 1 = 6%, Year 2 = 7.5%, Year 3 = 9%. Total SI on loan x: x(6 + 7.5 + 9)/100 = 8190.
Rs. 36400 is not listed among options A–D, so the answer is E) None of these.

solution.shortcut: Sum the three rates and solve: 22.5x/100 = 8190 ⟹ x = 36400.
solution.commonMistake: Using compound interest logic instead of simple interest for each year.

modelName: Simple Interest — Increasing rate each year

questionText: A person deposited $\text{Rs.}~400$ for 2 years, $\text{Rs.}~550$ for 4 years and $\text{Rs.}~1200$ for 6 years. He received the total simple interest of $\text{Rs.}~1020$. The rate of interest per annum is:

solution.shortcut: Sum P×T products, multiply by R/100, set equal to total SI.
solution.commonMistake: Adding periods incorrectly or forgetting one deposit.

modelName: Simple Interest — Multiple deposits, find rate

questionText: The simple interest on a sum of money will be $\text{Rs.}~600$ after 10 years. If the principal is trebled after 5 years, what will be the total interest at the end of the tenth year?

steps.explanation: SI = Rs. 600 in 10 years on original principal x. Rate R = 600×100/(x×10) = 6000/x %.
SI for first 5 years on x at rate R: SI₁ = x × (6000/x) × 5 / 100 = 300.
After 5 years principal is trebled to 3x. SI for last 5 years = 3x × (6000/x) × 5/100 = 900.
Total interest = 300 + 900.

solution.shortcut: SI doubles for first 5 yrs (= Rs. 300), trebled principal gives 3× SI in last 5 yrs (= Rs. 900). Total = 1200.
solution.commonMistake: Computing SI on trebled principal for all 10 years.

modelName: Simple Interest — Principal change mid-term

questionText: The simple interest on $\text{Rs.}~10$ for 4 months at the rate of 3 paise per rupee per month is:

steps.explanation: Rate = 3 paise per rupee per month = 3% per month. SI = P × R × T / 100.

solution.shortcut: Direct: 10 × 3 paise × 4 = 120 paise = Rs. 1.20.
solution.commonMistake: Converting the month-rate to annual rate unnecessarily and dividing by 12.

modelName: Simple Interest — Non-standard rate (paise per rupee per month)

questionText: An automobile financier claims to be lending money at simple interest, but he includes the interest every six months for calculating the principal. If he is charging an interest of $10\%$, the effective rate of interest becomes:

steps.explanation: Take principal = Rs. 100. SI for first 6 months at 10% p.a. = 100 × 10 × (1/2) / 100 = Rs. 5.
New principal for next 6 months = 100 + 5 = Rs. 105. SI for next 6 months = 105 × 10 × 0.5 / 100 = Rs. 5.25.
Total interest in 1 year = 5 + 5.25 = Rs. 10.25. Effective rate = 10.25%.

solution.shortcut: Like compound interest half-yearly: Effective rate = (1 + 0.05)² − 1 = 10.25%.
solution.commonMistake: Treating it as ordinary SI throughout the year.

modelName: Simple Interest — Effective rate with semi-annual compounding

questionText: A sum of money at simple interest amounts to $\text{Rs.}~815$ in 3 years and to $\text{Rs.}~854$ in 4 years. The sum is:

steps.explanation: SI for 1 year = 854 − 815 = Rs. 39.
SI for 3 years = 39 × 3 = Rs. 117.
Principal = Amount at 3 years − SI for 3 years = 815 − 117.

solution.shortcut: Annual SI = difference in amounts for 1-year gap; subtract 3-year SI from 3-year amount.
solution.commonMistake: Using the 4-year amount to subtract instead of the 3-year amount.

modelName: Simple Interest — Find principal from two amounts

questionText: A sum of money lent out at simple interest amounts to $\text{Rs.}~720$ after 2 years and to $\text{Rs.}~1020$ after a further period of 5 years. The sum is:

steps.explanation: SI for 5 years (from year 2 to year 7) = 1020 − 720 = Rs. 300.
SI for 2 years = 300 × 2 / 5 = Rs. 120.
Principal = 720 − 120.

solution.commonMistake: Treating 'further 5 years' as 5 years from the start rather than from year 2.

modelName: Simple Interest — Find principal from two amounts (non-consecutive start)

questionText: A sum of money amounts to $\text{Rs.}~5200$ in 5 years and to $\text{Rs.}~5680$ in 7 years at simple interest. The rate of interest per annum is:

steps.explanation: SI for 2 years = 5680 − 5200 = Rs. 480. So annual SI = Rs. 240.
SI for 5 years = 240 × 5 = Rs. 1200. Principal = 5200 − 1200 = Rs. 4000.

solution.commonMistake: Not subtracting 5-year SI to recover the principal.

modelName: Simple Interest — Find rate from two amounts

questionText: A sum of money becomes $\text{Rs.}~20925$ in 2 years and $\text{Rs.}~24412.50$ in 5 years. Find the rate of interest and the sum of money.

solution.commonMistake: Using 5-year amount directly to find principal without subtracting SI.

modelName: Simple Interest — Find principal and rate from two amounts

questionText: A certain sum of money at simple interest amounts to $\text{Rs.}~1012$ in $2\dfrac{1}{2}$ years and to $\text{Rs.}~1067.20$ in 4 years. The rate of interest per annum is:

steps.explanation: Principal = 1012 − 92 = Rs. 920.

solution.shortcut: Difference in amounts over 1.5 yrs gives per-year SI; back-calculate principal then rate.
solution.commonMistake: Computing SI for the wrong time interval.

modelName: Simple Interest — Find rate from two amounts (fractional years)

questionText: In how many years will a sum of money double itself at $18.75\%$ per annum simple interest?

steps.explanation: For a sum to double, SI = P. Let sum = Rs. x. Then T = SI × 100 / (P × R) = x × 100 / (x × 18.75).

solution.shortcut: $T = \dfrac{100}{R}$ when the sum doubles (SI = P).
solution.commonMistake: Confusing doubling time with tripling time.

modelName: Simple Interest — Time to double

questionText: At what rate percent of simple interest will a sum of money double itself in 12 years?

steps.explanation: For a sum to double, SI = P. Rate = SI × 100 / (P × T) = P × 100 / (P × 12) = 100/12.

solution.shortcut: $R = \dfrac{100}{T}$ when the sum doubles.
solution.commonMistake: Using T = 12 in the wrong formula.

modelName: Simple Interest — Rate to double

questionText: The rate at which a sum becomes four times itself in 15 years at S.I., will be:

steps.explanation: For a sum to become 4 times, SI = 3P. Let sum = x, so SI = 3x.

solution.shortcut: SI = (n−1)×P for n-times growth. R = (n−1)×100/T = 3×100/15 = 20%.
solution.commonMistake: Using SI = 4P instead of SI = 3P.

modelName: Simple Interest — Rate for n-times growth

questionText: A sum of money at a simple rate of interest $i_1$ doubles in 5 years. At another simple rate of interest $i_2$, it becomes three times in 12 years. Then the two rates of interest $i_1$ and $i_2$ respectively are:

steps.explanation: Doubles in 5 years: SI = P, so i₁ = 100/5 = 20%.
Trebles in 12 years: SI = 2P, so i₂ = 2×100/12 = 200/12 = 50/3%.

solution.shortcut: Doubles: i = 100/T. Trebles: i = 200/T.
solution.commonMistake: Using n times instead of (n−1) times the principal for SI.

modelName: Simple Interest — Two rates for different growth

questionText: A sum of money at simple interest doubles in 7 years. It will become four times in:

steps.explanation: Doubles in 7 years means SI = P in 7 years. Annual SI = P/7.
For the sum to become 4 times, SI needed = 3P. Time = 3P / (P/7) = 21 years.

solution.shortcut: If doubles in T years, becomes n-times in (n−1)×T years. For 4 times: 3×7 = 21 years.
solution.commonMistake: Thinking 4 times takes 4×7 = 28 years (wrong: should be 3 times the doubling time).

modelName: Simple Interest — Time for n-times growth

formulas.title: Time for n-times if doubles in T

questionText: A sum of money trebles itself in 15 years 6 months. In how many years would it double itself?

steps.explanation: Trebles means SI = 2P. Time T₁ = 15.5 years = 31/2 years. Rate R = 2P × 100 / (P × 31/2) = 400/31 %.
For doubling, SI = P. Time = P × 100 / (P × 400/31) = 100 × 31/400 = 31/4 years.

solution.shortcut: If trebles in T years, doubles in T/2 years. 15.5/2 = 7.75 years = 7 yrs 9 months.
solution.commonMistake: Using 15 years instead of 15.5 years.

modelName: Simple Interest — Time to double from trebling info

questionText: If a sum doubles in 16 years, how much will it be in 8 years?

steps.explanation: Doubles in 16 years: R = 100/16 = 25/4 % = 6.25%. Annual SI = 6.25% of P.
SI in 8 years = P × (25/4) × 8 / 100 = P/2. Amount = P + P/2 = 3P/2 = 1½ times P.

solution.shortcut: In half the doubling time, amount = 1 + ½ = 1½ times.
solution.commonMistake: Thinking half the time means half the final amount instead of half the SI.

modelName: Simple Interest — Amount at half the doubling time

questionText: Consider the following statements. If a sum of money is lent at simple interest, then the: (1) money gets doubled in 5 years if the rate of interest is $16\dfrac{2}{3}\%$; (2) money gets doubled in 5 years if the rate of interest is $20\%$; (3) money becomes four times in 10 years if it gets doubled in 5 years. Of these statements:

steps.explanation: Check Statement 1: R = 100/T = 100/5 = 20%, not 16⅔%. So Statement 1 is FALSE.
Check Statement 2: R = 20% gives doubling in 5 years. TRUE.
Check Statement 3: If doubles in 5 years, for 4-times: SI = 3P, time = 3×5 = 15 years, not 10. So Statement 3 is FALSE.

solution.shortcut: Only statement 2 is correct.
solution.commonMistake: Assuming that doubling in 5 years means quadrupling in 10 years under SI (that is CI logic).

modelName: Simple Interest — Statement verification

questionText: The simple interest on a sum of money at $8\%$ per annum for 6 years is half the sum. The sum is:

steps.explanation: Let sum = x. SI = x/2. Check: SI = x × 8 × 6 / 100 = 48x/100 = 12x/25.
But SI is stated to be x/2. Check 12x/25 = x/2 ⟹ 24x = 25x ⟹ contradiction. The given conditions are inconsistent — the data is inadequate/contradictory.

solution.shortcut: 8% for 6 years gives SI = 48% of sum, not 50%. No consistent answer exists.
solution.commonMistake: Trying to compute a specific numerical answer from internally inconsistent data.

modelName: Simple Interest — Consistency check

questionText: At what rate percent per annum will the simple interest on a sum of money be $\dfrac{2}{5}$ of the amount in 10 years?

steps.explanation: Let sum = x. SI = (2/5)x, T = 10 years. Rate = SI × 100 / (P × T).

solution.commonMistake: Using '2/5 of the amount' to mean 2/5 of (P+SI) instead of 2/5 of P.

modelName: Simple Interest — Find rate given SI as fraction of principal

questionText: In how much time would the simple interest on a certain sum be $0.125$ times the principal at $10\%$ per annum?

solution.commonMistake: Confusing 0.125 with 1/4 instead of 1/8.

modelName: Simple Interest — Find time given SI as fraction of principal

questionText: How long will it take a sum of money invested at $5\%$ p.a. S.I. to increase its value by $40\%$?

steps.explanation: Increase of 40% means SI = 0.40 P = 2P/5. T = SI × 100 / (P × R) = (2P/5) × 100 / (P × 5).

solution.commonMistake: Using 40 instead of 40/100 for SI as a fraction of P.

modelName: Simple Interest — Time to increase by percentage

questionText: A sum of money becomes $\dfrac{7}{6}$ of itself in 3 years at a certain rate of simple interest. The rate per annum is:

steps.explanation: Amount = 7x/6. SI = 7x/6 − x = x/6. T = 3 years.

solution.commonMistake: Using 7/6 as SI instead of finding SI = 7/6 − 1 = 1/6 of P.

modelName: Simple Interest — Rate from fractional amount

questionText: If the simple interest for 6 years be equal to $30\%$ of the principal, it will be equal to the principal after:

steps.explanation: SI = 30% of P in 6 years. Rate = 30 × 100 / (100 × 6) = 5% per annum.
For SI = P (100% of P), time T = 100/R = 100/5.

solution.shortcut: Annual SI rate = 5%; for SI = P, T = 100/5 = 20 years.
solution.commonMistake: Doubling the 30% period (6×2 = 12) to get 12 years, which is wrong.

modelName: Simple Interest — Time for SI to equal principal

questionText: Simple interest on a certain sum at a certain annual rate of interest is $\dfrac{1}{9}$ of the sum. If the numbers representing rate percent and time in years be equal, then the rate of interest is:

steps.explanation: Let sum = x. SI = x/9. Let rate = R% and time = R years (since they are equal).

solution.commonMistake: Not squaring R when rate = time.

modelName: Simple Interest — Equal rate and time, find rate

questionText: Simple interest on a certain amount is $\dfrac{9}{16}$ of the principal. If the numbers representing the rate of interest in percent and time in years be equal, then time for which the principal is lent out is:

steps.explanation: SI = 9x/16. Let R = T. Then (9x/16) = x × R² / 100, so R² = 900/16.

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to take square root of R².

modelName: Simple Interest — Equal rate and time, find time

questionText: A lends $\text{Rs.}~2500$ to B and a certain sum to C at the same time at $7\%$ p.a. simple interest. If after 4 years, A altogether receives $\text{Rs.}~1120$ as interest from B and C, then the sum lent to C is:

steps.explanation: Let sum lent to C = Rs. x. Interest from B = 2500 × 7 × 4 / 100 = Rs. 700.

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that A lends to both B and C at the same rate.

modelName: Simple Interest — Two borrowers same rate

questionText: Two equal sums of money were lent at simple interest at $11\%$ p.a. for $3\dfrac{1}{2}$ years and $4\dfrac{1}{2}$ years respectively. If the difference in interests for two periods was $\text{Rs.}~412.50$, then each sum is:

steps.explanation: Let each sum = Rs. x. Difference in SI = x × 11 × (9/2 − 7/2) / 100 = x × 11 × 1 / 100 = 11x/100.

solution.shortcut: Difference = SI for 1 extra year = 11x/100 = 412.50 ⟹ x = 3750.
solution.commonMistake: Computing total SI instead of difference.

modelName: Simple Interest — Difference in SI for different periods

questionText: If the simple interest on a certain sum for 15 months at $7\dfrac{1}{2}\%$ per annum exceeds the simple interest on the same sum for 8 months at $12\dfrac{1}{2}\%$ per annum by $\text{Rs.}~32.50$, then the sum (in Rs.) is:

solution.shortcut: Convert months to years, set up equation, solve for x.
solution.commonMistake: Not converting months to years when applying the SI formula.

modelName: Simple Interest — Difference in SI with different rates and times

questionText: Sujata invested $\text{Rs.}~7500$ at simple interest @ $11\%$ p.c.p.a. She further invested some amount at simple interest @ $15\%$ p.c.p.a. Total interest earned at the end of the year became $12\%$ p.c.p.a. Find the amount invested at the rate of $15\%$ p.c.p.a.

steps.explanation: Let additional sum = Rs. x. Total interest = 11% of 7500 + 15% of x = 12% of (7500 + x).

solution.commonMistake: Setting 11% of total equal to 12% without accounting for both rates.

modelName: Simple Interest — Mixture of two investment rates

questionText: A man invests a certain sum of money at $6\%$ p.a. simple interest and another sum at $7\%$ p.a. simple interest. His income from interest after 2 years was $\text{Rs.}~354$. One-fourth of the first sum is equal to one-fifth of the second sum. The total sum invested was:

steps.explanation: Let sums be x and y. x/4 = y/5 ⟹ 5x = 4y ⟹ y = 5x/4.

solution.shortcut: Use x/4 = y/5 to express y in terms of x, substitute in SI equation.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to multiply by 2 years in the interest equation.

modelName: Simple Interest — Two sums with constraint on ratio

questionText: Rahul borrowed a sum of $\text{Rs.}~1150$ from Amit at $6\%$ p.c.p.a. simple interest for 3 years. He then added some more money to the borrowed sum and lent it to Sachin for the same time at $9\%$ p.c.p.a. simple interest. If Rahul gains $\text{Rs.}~274.95$ by way of interest on the borrowed sum as well as his own amount from the whole transaction, what is the sum lent by him to Sachin?

steps.explanation: Let the extra money added by Rahul = Rs. x. Total lent to Sachin = (1150 + x).
Interest Rahul earns from Sachin = (1150 + x) × 9 × 3 / 100 = 27(1150 + x)/100.
Interest Rahul pays to Amit = 1150 × 6 × 3 / 100 = Rs. 207. Net gain = SI_Sachin − 207 = 274.95.

solution.shortcut: Net gain = Interest earned − Interest paid. Solve for total sum lent to Sachin.
solution.commonMistake: Computing gross interest to Sachin without subtracting the interest paid to Amit.

modelName: Simple Interest — Borrowed and re-lent at different rate

questionText: A person invested some amount at the rate of $12\%$ simple interest and a certain amount at the rate of $10\%$ simple interest. He received yearly interest of $\text{Rs.}~130$. But if he had interchanged the amounts invested, he would have received $\text{Rs.}~4$ more as interest. How much did he invest at $12\%$ simple interest?

steps.explanation: Let amount at 12% = Rs. x, at 10% = Rs. y. Then 0.12x + 0.10y = 130 ⟹ 6x + 5y = 6500 ...(i).
If interchanged: 0.10x + 0.12y = 134 ⟹ 5x + 6y = 6700 ...(ii).
Adding (i) and (ii): 11(x + y) = 13200 ⟹ x + y = 1200. Subtracting: −x + y = 200. So y = 700, x = 500.

solution.shortcut: Two equations from original and swapped cases; solve simultaneously.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to add Rs. 4 to get the second interest amount.

modelName: Simple Interest — Two investments with swapped amounts

questionText: A person borrowed $\text{Rs.}~500$ @ $3\%$ per annum S.I. and $\text{Rs.}~600$ @ $4\dfrac{1}{2}\%$ per annum on the agreement that the whole sum will be returned only when the total interest becomes $\text{Rs.}~126$. The number of years after which the borrowed sum is to be returned is:

solution.shortcut: Total annual SI = 15 + 27 = 42; years = 126/42 = 3.
solution.commonMistake: Using 9/2 instead of 4.5 leading to arithmetic errors.

modelName: Simple Interest — Two loans, find time

questionText: Two equal sums of money are lent at the same time at $8\%$ and $7\%$ per annum simple interest. The former is recovered 6 months earlier than the latter and the amount in each case is $\text{Rs.}~2560$. The sum and the time for which the sums of money are lent out are:

steps.explanation: Let each sum = x. 8% sum is recovered in (T − 0.5) yrs, 7% sum in T yrs. Both amounts = 2560.
From (i): 100x − 4x + 8xT = 256000. Equating (i) and (ii): 96x + 8xT = 100x + 7xT ⟹ xT = 4x ⟹ T = 4.

solution.shortcut: Set up two amount equations, subtract to find T, then x.
solution.commonMistake: Swapping which rate corresponds to which time period.

modelName: Simple Interest — Two equal sums recovered at different times

questionText: A lent $\text{Rs.}~5000$ to B for 2 years and $\text{Rs.}~3000$ to C for 4 years on simple interest at the same rate of interest and received $\text{Rs.}~2200$ in all from both of them as interest. The rate of interest per annum is:

steps.formula: SI = \frac{P_1 R T_1 + P_2 R T_2}{100}

solution.commonMistake: Using different rates for B and C.

modelName: Simple Interest — Two loans find rate
